summary:
Sahul was the ancient supercontinent that joined present-day Australia, New Guinea, and Tasmania into a single landmass during periods of lower sea level associated with glacial maxima. At its greatest extent, Sahul offered continuous terrestrial passage between these now-separate regions, enabling the spread of human populations and shared faunal communities across what is today a fragmented island geography. As global temperatures rose following the Last Glacial Maximum and the post-glacial sea level rise accelerated through the period roughly 10,000 to 8,000 BCE, the land connections binding Sahul together were progressively inundated, gradually severing the populations of Australia, New Guinea, and Tasmania from one another.

The submersion of the Sahul land connections had lasting consequences for the human communities living across the region. Tasmania, the southernmost extension of Sahul, became fully isolated from mainland Australia by approximately 10,000 to 9,000 BCE as rising Bass Strait waters completed their encroachment. The Aboriginal Tasmanians who remained found themselves cut off not only geographically but culturally, and their subsequent millennia of isolated development produced a material culture and social life that diverged markedly from that of mainland Aboriginal Australians. Similarly, the narrowing and eventual submersion of the Torres Strait connection reshaped relationships between New Guinea highlanders and their Australian counterparts. The event thus represents not merely a geographic transition but a human one, as communities adapted to an increasingly archipelagic world.
